2. Upgrade: “Poor people suffer more during heatwaves.” using distributional effects.

The distributional effects of extreme heat fall most heavily on low-income households.

3. Upgrade: “Some places may need to move away from the coast.” using managed retreat.

Managed retreat may become necessary where repeated coastal flooding makes protection unrealistic.

4. Upgrade: “The transition may create new dependencies.” using critical minerals.

Dependence on critical minerals creates a new category of energy-security risk.

5. Upgrade: “Cities need more trees and shade.” using urban tree canopy.

Expanding the urban tree canopy can reduce heat exposure and improve public space.

6. Upgrade: “Power lines are delaying renewable projects.” using grid bottlenecks.

Grid bottlenecks are delaying otherwise viable renewable-energy projects.

7. Upgrade: “Governments should prepare for several risks together.” using compound risks.

Climate planning must account for compound risks rather than isolated hazards.

8. Upgrade: “Buildings need to be upgraded.” using building retrofit.

Building retrofit can reduce both emissions and heat vulnerability.

9. Upgrade: “People need warnings before extreme weather.” using early-warning systems.

Early-warning systems give households and public services time to prepare.

10. Upgrade: “Climate policy must be fair.” using just transition.

A just transition distributes costs and opportunities more fairly.

11. Upgrade: “The system must cope with shocks.” using grid resilience.

Grid resilience determines whether essential services continue during disruption.

12. Upgrade: “Countries should not depend too much on imported fuel.” using energy independence.

Greater energy independence can reduce exposure to geopolitical supply shocks.

13. Upgrade: “Adaptation projects do not receive enough money.” using adaptation finance.

Adaptation finance remains far below the level required by vulnerable countries.

14. Upgrade: “The government should explain the costs clearly.” using transparent communication.

Transparent communication is essential when policies change household costs.

15. Upgrade: “Policies for housing and energy should support each other.” using policy coherence.

Policy coherence is needed across housing, transport and energy systems.

9. IELTS Speaking

Part 1 · 15 questions

PART 1 · 1

Do you try to save energy at home?

Suggested phrasal verbs
cut backpay off
Model answer · 89 words

I try to cut back unnecessary electricity use by switching off lights, reducing standby power and adjusting heating sensibly. Small savings will not solve climate change alone, but they can lower energy bills and reduce peak demand. Better building insulation would probably pay off more than constant personal vigilance. I prefer practical changes that improve comfort as well as efficiency, because habits are easier to maintain when they do not feel like permanent punishment. I also monitor whether efficiency changes actually reduce use rather than merely shifting it elsewhere.

PART 1 · 2

Have your energy bills changed recently?

Suggested phrasal verbs
ride outcut back
Model answer · 87 words

My energy costs have become less predictable, especially during unusually hot or cold periods. When prices rise, households try to cut back, but essential heating and cooling cannot simply disappear. Savings and fixed tariffs may help people ride out a temporary supply shock, although low-income families need direct support. The experience shows that energy security is not an abstract national concept; it affects ordinary decisions about food, transport and indoor comfort. Stable policy is important because households cannot plan around prices that change with every geopolitical crisis.

PART 1 · 3

Would you install solar panels?

Suggested phrasal verbs
switch overpay off
Model answer · 83 words

I would consider solar panels if the building, ownership rules and financing made sense. They could help me switch over part of my electricity use to renewable energy, especially if combined with battery storage. The investment would need several years to pay off, so reliable policy matters. I would also want clear information about maintenance, recycling and whether surplus power could be sold back to the power grid. Shared or community solar could make the option available to renters as well as homeowners.

PART 1 · 4

Do you use air conditioning?

Suggested phrasal verbs
cool downcut back
Model answer · 85 words

I use air conditioning only during serious extreme heat, because it can quickly cool down a room and protect sleep. However, I try to cut back demand through shade, ventilation and fans first. Air conditioning is sometimes treated as a luxury, but during dangerous heat it becomes a health measure. The problem is ensuring equitable access while avoiding a cycle in which rising cooling demand increases pressure on the grid. Public buildings should provide safe cooling so protection does not depend entirely on private income.

PART 1 · 5

Are heatwaves common where you live?

Suggested phrasal verbs
build instep up
Model answer · 80 words

Heatwaves have become more noticeable, and buildings are not always designed for them. Cities need to build in shade, ventilation and access to water rather than relying only on emergency advice. Authorities should also step up heat action plans and open cooling centres before hospitals become overwhelmed. People often underestimate heat because it lacks the dramatic images of a storm, yet its health effects can be severe. Employers and schools also need clear thresholds for reducing outdoor activity. Preparedness matters.

PART 1 · 6

Do you pay attention to weather warnings?

Suggested phrasal verbs
ride outstep up
Model answer · 87 words

I pay attention when warnings concern floods, severe storms or dangerous heat. Good early-warning systems give people time to prepare and help services step up before conditions worsen. However, warnings work only if the public understands what action to take. A family may ride out a short disruption more safely when it has water, medication and charged devices. Repeated vague alerts can reduce trust, so communication should remain specific and proportionate. Neighbours may need special support when age, disability or language makes warnings harder to act upon.

PART 1 · 7

Would you buy an electric car?

Suggested phrasal verbs
switch overpay off
Model answer · 87 words

I might switch over to an electric car when charging is convenient and the price becomes more competitive. electric vehicles can reduce urban pollution and fossil-fuel demand, but their benefit depends on the electricity mix and battery supply chain. The purchase may pay off through lower running costs, although people without private parking face practical barriers. Public transport and compact cities remain more important than replacing every car with a different type of car. Charging should be managed intelligently so vehicles support rather than destabilise peak demand.

PART 1 · 8

Do you think your home is climate-resilient?

Suggested phrasal verbs
build inshore up
Model answer · 79 words

My home was designed for older weather patterns, so it is only partly resilient. Better shading, drainage and insulation could shore up protection against heat and heavy rain. New construction should build in future climate conditions from the beginning, because later building retrofit is more expensive. Resilience also depends on the neighbourhood: one protected house still suffers if roads, electricity or water systems fail. Insurance and mortgage systems will increasingly reflect whether adaptation has been taken seriously. Preparation matters.

PART 1 · 9

Would you live near a wind farm?

Suggested phrasal verbs
build outspread out
Model answer · 79 words

I would live near a well-planned wind farm if noise, landscape and wildlife impacts were assessed honestly. Countries need to build out wind power, and geographically dispersed generation can spread out infrastructure risk. Local communities should receive clear benefits, such as lower bills or shared ownership. Consultation matters because describing every objection as ignorance usually creates resistance rather than social legitimacy. Developers should also protect wildlife corridors and compensate communities for visible landscape change. Local consent would remain essential.

PART 1 · 10

Do trees make cities more comfortable?

Suggested phrasal verbs
cool downbuild in
Model answer · 82 words

Trees make a major difference because they provide shade, release moisture and help streets cool down. An urban tree canopy also improves air quality and makes walking more pleasant. Cities should build in long-term maintenance rather than plant trees for a photograph and then let them die. Greening must reach poorer districts too, since heat exposure often follows existing distributional effects. Trees require water, soil and space, so urban greening is infrastructure rather than decoration. Long-term maintenance determines whether those benefits last.

PART 1 · 11

Have you experienced flooding?

Suggested phrasal verbs
ride outshore up
Model answer · 79 words

I have seen local streets flood after intense rain, although my home was not seriously affected. Temporary barriers helped residents ride out the event, but long-term protection requires drainage, maintenance and better planning. Authorities need to shore up flood resilience before another emergency. Flooding demonstrates how one hazard can disrupt transport, electricity, schools and healthcare at the same time. Updated maps should guide construction because historical averages no longer describe future risk. Neighbourhood-level maintenance therefore deserves consistent public funding.

PART 1 · 12

Do you support nuclear power?

Suggested phrasal verbs
phase outshore up
Model answer · 79 words

I support keeping safe nuclear plants where they can shore up low-carbon reliability, especially while grids expand. However, nuclear projects are expensive and slow, so they should not be used as an excuse to phase out renewable investment. The best mix depends on national conditions. Waste, construction risk and public confidence require honest discussion rather than slogans from either side. Transparent planning is essential because delayed projects can make an apparently cheap option expensive. Diversification would still remain essential.

PART 1 · 13

Would you join a community energy project?

Suggested phrasal verbs
roll outspread out
Model answer · 80 words

I would join a local project if residents could roll out a shared solar programme, storage or efficiency improvements with transparent costs. Community ownership can spread out benefits and build support for the energy transition. It may also help apartment residents who cannot install individual panels. The project would still need professional maintenance and fair rules, because enthusiasm cannot replace reliable engineering. Local ownership can also keep part of the financial return within the community. Clear governance would remain essential.

PART 1 · 14

Is public transport part of climate policy?

Suggested phrasal verbs
scale upswitch over
Model answer · 81 words

Public transport is central because cities cannot switch over millions of journeys through private electric cars alone. Governments should scale up reliable buses, rail and safe cycling while improving affordability. This reduces emissions, congestion and household costs at the same time. The policy works only when services are frequent enough that people can realistically change behaviour rather than being lectured from a platform with one bus per hour. Clean transport policy should improve mobility for people who cannot drive as well.

PART 1 · 15

Do you discuss climate change with other people?

Suggested phrasal verbs
build instep up
Model answer · 83 words

I discuss it when the conversation is practical rather than purely ideological. I try to investigate specific claims about costs, energy systems or local risks instead of repeating dramatic headlines. I try to build in local evidence, and people are more willing to step up when solutions feel credible and fair. Transparent communication should acknowledge uncertainty and trade-offs while making clear that delay also creates costs. Concrete local examples usually make the discussion more productive than arguments about distant dates. Specific evidence helps.

Part 3 · 15 questions

PART 3 · 1

Should mitigation or adaptation receive more funding?

Suggested phrasal verbs
step upscale up
Model answer · 127 words

Governments must fund both because they address different timescales. climate mitigation limits future warming, while climate adaptation reduces harm that is already unavoidable. Wealthy countries should scale up clean-energy investment and step up adaptation finance, particularly for vulnerable regions. Treating the two as competitors is misleading: weak mitigation makes adaptation progressively harder and more expensive. resource allocation should consider immediate risk, long-term emissions and the capacity of local institutions. A balanced portfolio might protect hospitals from heat today while transforming electricity and transport for the future. Adaptation spending should also be evaluated for avoided losses, which are less visible than new construction but often economically substantial. Governments should publish separate budgets, because combining them in one headline can hide whether either side is receiving enough sustained investment.

PART 3 · 2

Can renewable energy improve national security?

Suggested phrasal verbs
build outspread out
Model answer · 124 words

Renewables can improve energy security by reducing exposure to imported fuels and geopolitical supply shock. Governments that build out domestic wind, solar and storage can spread out generation across many sites, making one disruption less damaging. However, clean systems create new dependencies on grids, digital control and critical minerals. Security therefore requires supply-chain resilience, storage and diversified manufacturing. Renewable energy is not automatically independent, but it reduces the recurring vulnerability created by global fossil-fuel prices. Cybersecurity and spare equipment become more important as the system grows more digital and interconnected. Regional interconnection can also reduce vulnerability, although agreements need fair emergency rules so one country does not protect itself by exporting shortages to neighbours. Domestic manufacturing can further reduce exposure to fragile supply chains.

PART 3 · 3

Why are electricity grids so important?

Suggested phrasal verbs
shore uphold back
Model answer · 121 words

The grid connects generation, storage and consumers, so it is the backbone of electrification. Weak transmission capacity can hold back renewable projects even when turbines or panels are ready. Governments must shore up grid resilience against heat, storms, cyberattacks and equipment shortages. Grids also need power-system flexibility to balance variable supply and new demand from electric vehicles and heat pumps. Building generation without networks is like constructing factories without roads: the productive assets exist, but the system cannot move their output where it is needed. Planning rules and faster permitting should improve speed without removing environmental review or public consultation. Skilled technicians, transformers and planning capacity are as important as finance; infrastructure cannot be accelerated simply by announcing a larger target.

PART 3 · 4

Should governments subsidise household energy upgrades?

Suggested phrasal verbs
roll outpay off
Model answer · 123 words

Subsidies are justified because building retrofit creates public benefits as well as private savings. Governments can roll out grants or low-interest loans for insulation, heat pumps and solar. These investments may pay off through lower energy bills, reduced emissions and less pressure on the grid. Programmes should target low-income and inefficient homes first, since affluent owners are more able to invest without support. Quality control matters: poor installation can waste money and damage public confidence. Support should be simple enough that households without specialist knowledge can actually use it. Renters require protection because landlords receive the asset value while tenants often pay the energy bills and endure the disruption of poor-quality housing. Tenant protections should prevent upgrades from becoming a reason for eviction.

PART 3 · 5

How should cities prepare for extreme heat?

Suggested phrasal verbs
cool downbuild in
Model answer · 122 words

Cities need a combination of emergency planning and permanent design. Trees, shade, reflective surfaces and ventilation help neighbourhoods cool down, while cooling centres protect people during dangerous periods. New buildings should build in passive cooling and safe indoor temperatures. heat action plans must connect forecasts with healthcare, schools and outreach to isolated residents. Cities should map exposure because poorer areas often have less urban tree canopy. Adaptation becomes effective when it is treated as normal infrastructure rather than a temporary response to each record summer. Night-time heat deserves particular attention because it prevents recovery and increases health risk. Heat policy should include labour standards, school design and public-health surveillance, since exposure is shaped by work and housing as much as outdoor temperature.

PART 3 · 6

Is managed retreat sometimes necessary?

Suggested phrasal verbs
step upbuild in
Model answer · 125 words

managed retreat may be necessary where repeated flooding or sea-level rise makes protection financially or physically unrealistic. Governments should step up planning early and build in compensation, housing and community participation. Waiting until properties become uninsurable leaves households trapped. Retreat is politically difficult because places carry identity and history, but unlimited protection can transfer huge costs to the public. A fair process should use updated risk assessment, protect renters as well as owners and avoid forcing disadvantaged communities to move without alternatives. Governments should preserve social networks by moving communities together where possible rather than treating retreat as an individual property transaction. Decisions should be made before repeated disasters destroy property values, because late action leaves governments paying emergency compensation without delivering a viable future.

PART 3 · 7

Does adaptation reduce pressure to cut emissions?

Suggested phrasal verbs
lock inphase out
Model answer · 119 words

Adaptation should never become an excuse to continue high emissions. Some actors argue that societies can simply adjust, but severe warming can exceed physical and financial limits. New fossil infrastructure may lock in emissions for decades, making later change harder. Governments still need to phase out high-carbon systems while investing in resilience. Adaptation and mitigation reinforce one another: efficient buildings reduce heat risk and energy demand, while clean power supports cooling without increasing emissions. The danger lies in presenting adaptation as permission to abandon climate targets. Failure to reduce emissions would eventually make some adaptation measures technically or financially impossible. Governments should publish sectoral pathways so adaptation investment complements rather than replaces legally credible emissions reductions. That distinction matters.

PART 3 · 8

How can energy transitions remain fair?

Suggested phrasal verbs
switch overstep up
Model answer · 128 words

A just transition requires affordable energy, worker support and regional investment. When industries switch over from fossil fuels, governments should step up retraining, income protection and new infrastructure before closures occur. Low-income households need help with upfront costs, not only future savings. Policymakers should examine distributional effects because carbon prices or network charges can be regressive. Fairness also means access to clean cooking and reliable electricity in poorer countries. The transition gains social legitimacy when benefits are visible and costs are not concentrated on people with the least influence. Worker participation can identify realistic transition paths and reduce resistance created by decisions imposed from above. Public procurement and industrial policy can create replacement employment before fossil facilities close, making change more credible than promises of future green jobs.

PART 3 · 9

Should countries keep investing in fossil fuels for security?

Suggested phrasal verbs
lock inride out
Model answer · 122 words

Temporary reserves may help countries ride out acute shortages, but new long-lived fossil projects can lock in emissions and may arrive too late to solve the immediate crisis. Security should focus on diversified supply, efficiency, grids, storage and clean generation. Continued fossil dependence exposes consumers to global price volatility. Some gas infrastructure may remain during transition, yet every project should face a realistic demand test. Energy security is strongest when households need less imported fuel, not when governments promise unlimited domestic extraction. Public money should not protect assets that are likely to become uneconomic while cheaper resilience measures remain unfunded. Existing infrastructure should be assessed against future demand and climate policy, not protected indefinitely because its construction has already created political influence.

PART 3 · 10

Can batteries solve renewable intermittency?

Suggested phrasal verbs
shore upscale up
Model answer · 119 words

battery storage can shore up short-term reliability by moving electricity from sunny or windy periods into peak demand. Countries should scale up grid-scale storage, but batteries are not the only solution. Transmission, demand response, hydro, interconnection and flexible generation all contribute. Different storage durations serve different needs, and seasonal gaps remain difficult. The sensible approach is a portfolio rather than one miraculous technology. Batteries are powerful because deployment is fast, but policy should also address recycling, fire safety and mineral supply. Storage markets also need rules that reward reliability and flexibility rather than only installed capacity. Long-duration technologies may become valuable as renewable shares rise, so research and demonstration should continue alongside commercial battery deployment. System diversity therefore matters.

PART 3 · 11

How should adaptation finance be distributed?

Suggested phrasal verbs
step upbuild in
Model answer · 120 words

Funding should prioritise vulnerability, exposure and local capacity rather than only political influence. Wealthy countries need to step up international adaptation finance, while programmes should build in local participation and maintenance. Large barriers may be necessary, but small investments in warnings, health systems and water management can save lives quickly. Grants are often better than loans for countries already carrying heavy debt. Donors should measure outcomes and strengthen institutions instead of funding impressive projects that communities cannot operate after consultants leave. Finance should reach cities and local organisations because they often implement adaptation even when national plans receive the publicity. Local organisations should also receive multi-year support, because resilience depends on maintenance and trust rather than one short funding cycle.

PART 3 · 12

Do climate policies threaten economic growth?

Suggested phrasal verbs
scale upswitch over
Model answer · 118 words

Poorly designed policy can create costs, but climate damage and fossil-price shocks also weaken growth. Economies that scale up efficiency, grids and clean industry may gain innovation and energy security. The challenge is helping firms and workers switch over without abrupt disruption. cost-benefit analysis should compare climate policy with the cost of inaction, not with an imaginary world where extreme weather is free. Growth also needs to be examined through health, resilience and shared prosperity, not only short-term output. The transition can support growth, but distribution and timing determine whether citizens experience opportunity or insecurity. Stable policy can reduce investment risk and household costs, whereas repeated reversals make both clean and conventional energy projects more expensive. Consistency matters.

PART 3 · 13

What role should nuclear power play?

Suggested phrasal verbs
shore upbuild out
Model answer · 125 words

Nuclear power can shore up low-carbon electricity where countries already possess skills, regulation and public acceptance. It may complement efforts to build out wind, solar and storage, especially in systems needing firm generation. However, new reactors often face high costs and long delays. The technology should be assessed by local conditions rather than ideology. Life extensions may offer value, while poorly managed megaprojects can crowd out faster options. Waste, security and cooling-water risks require serious oversight. Independent regulators are necessary because political pressure can conceal cost overruns and safety problems. Governments should compare nuclear projects with grids, efficiency and storage on the same timeline rather than using idealised costs from different planning assumptions. Maintenance budgets must also cover the full operating life of the plant.

PART 3 · 14

How should governments communicate climate risk?

Suggested phrasal verbs
step upbuild in
Model answer · 119 words

Communication should be specific, local and linked to action. Governments must step up warnings during emergencies but also build in long-term education about heat, floods and energy systems. Constant catastrophe language can exhaust people, while false reassurance leaves them unprepared. transparent communication should explain uncertainty, identify vulnerable groups and state what institutions are doing. Trust grows when advice is consistent with visible investment in infrastructure, not when citizens are told to change light bulbs while policy remains contradictory. Communication is more credible when agencies publish progress and admit where plans remain incomplete. Public agencies should work with trusted local institutions, because national messages may not reach people who are socially isolated or sceptical of central government. Local trust matters.

PART 3 · 15

What would a resilient energy system look like?

Suggested phrasal verbs
spread outride out
Model answer · 121 words

A resilient system would combine diverse generation, strong grids, storage, efficiency and emergency planning. Assets would be spread out geographically, reducing dependence on one fuel or facility. Communities could ride out short disruptions through local backup and flexible demand. The system would also protect low-income households from extreme prices. grid resilience, cyber security and spare equipment matter as much as generation capacity. Resilience does not mean complete self-sufficiency; it means failures remain limited, recoverable and socially manageable. Cross-border interconnection can improve security, provided countries avoid creating a new single point of dependence. Recovery plans should prioritise hospitals, communications and water systems, while transparent restoration rules prevent politically connected areas receiving preferential treatment. Resilience planning should be tested through regular emergency exercises.

10. Five IELTS Writing Task 2 topics

Before writing: check that each body paragraph has a clear topic sentence, explanation, development and a relevant consequence or example. Your position must remain consistent from the introduction to the conclusion.
TASK 2 · 1

Governments should subsidise household insulation and heat pumps even if this requires higher public spending. To what extent do you agree or disagree?

Optional collocation bank
building insulationheat pumpsswitch overclean electricitypower gridpay offenergy billspublic confidencegrid resilience
Model essay · 341 words

Household insulation and heat pumps can reduce emissions, energy demand and exposure to volatile fuel prices. I therefore agree that governments should subsidise them, even when this requires significant public expenditure.

The main justification is that private decisions create public benefits. Better building insulation lowers household demand during winter peaks, while heat pumps help countries switch over from fossil heating to clean electricity. What individual homeowners cannot capture is the value of reduced pressure on the wider power grid. Subsidies can therefore correct an investment gap. Support is also necessary for fairness. Efficient equipment may pay off over several years, but low-income households cannot always finance the initial cost. Without grants, the transition benefits affluent owners first while renters continue paying high energy bills. Programmes should prioritise inefficient homes and protect tenants from rent increases or eviction after upgrades.

The disadvantages are cost and quality risk. Poor installation can reduce performance, and generous subsidies may raise contractor prices. Governments should use accredited installers, inspections and simple consumer guarantees. Were subsidies linked to verified energy savings, waste would be easier to control. Public support should also be predictable. Repeatedly opening and closing schemes damages supply chains and public confidence. Training, low-interest finance and neighbourhood-level delivery can reduce administrative barriers.

Subsidies should also be coordinated with grid planning, because rapid electrification without local upgrades may create congestion. Time-of-use tariffs and smart controls can help heat pumps operate when electricity is abundant. The programme would then support both household comfort and grid resilience. A successful subsidy should therefore be judged by verified reductions in energy use, lower household costs and improved winter and summer comfort. Public reporting can identify whether support is reaching inefficient homes or merely rewarding households that would have invested anyway. This would strengthen democratic accountability and help governments adjust grant levels over time.

In conclusion: insulation and heat-pump subsidies are justified because they produce climate, security and health benefits beyond the household. Higher spending is acceptable when support is targeted, quality-controlled and evaluated through measurable reductions in bills and demand.

TASK 2 · 2

Some people believe renewable energy guarantees national energy security, while others argue that it creates new forms of dependence. Discuss both views and give your opinion.

Optional collocation bank
solar powerwind powerbuild outfossil dependencespread outenergy storagecritical mineralsscale uptransmission capacity
Model essay · 324 words

Renewable energy can strengthen national security by reducing dependence on imported fuels, yet it also creates new infrastructure and supply-chain vulnerabilities. In my view, renewables improve security overall, provided that countries invest in grids, storage and diversified manufacturing.

The strongest advantage is protection from global fuel shocks. Domestic solar power and wind power have no imported fuel price, so governments can build out generation and reduce fossil dependence. A decentralised system can also spread out physical risk rather than relying on a few large plants or pipelines. However, renewable output varies with weather. Weak grids and insufficient energy storage may create reliability problems. Clean technologies also depend on critical minerals, batteries and imported components. What changes is not the existence of dependence, but its form. Countries may become less exposed to oil and gas while relying on concentrated mineral processing.

The solution is diversification. Governments should scale up transmission capacity, storage, recycling and demand response. Strategic reserves and international partnerships can strengthen supply-chain resilience. Not only should countries expand clean generation, but they should also retain enough technical capacity to manage disruptions. Energy efficiency provides another layer of security because lower demand reduces exposure to every supply problem.

Regional interconnection can further improve security by allowing countries to share electricity during shortages. However, emergency agreements must prevent wealthier markets from purchasing all available supply. Security is stronger when cooperation has transparent rules rather than relying on improvised competition during a crisis. Domestic manufacturing may reduce some vulnerabilities, but complete self-sufficiency would be expensive and unrealistic. Countries should combine strategic reserves with diversified trade and common technical standards. This approach would allow suppliers to be replaced more easily when one region experiences political conflict, natural disaster or industrial disruption.

In conclusion: renewable energy does not guarantee complete independence. Nevertheless, a well-designed clean system is more secure than continued reliance on internationally traded fossil fuels, because its risks can be diversified through infrastructure, storage and cooperation.

TASK 2 · 3

Cities are investing heavily in trees, shade and cooling centres to respond to extreme heat. Do the advantages outweigh the disadvantages?

Optional collocation bank
extreme heaturban tree canopycool downcooling centresheatwave deathsequitable accesspassive coolingheat action plansclimate mitigation
Model essay · 312 words

Cities are increasingly using trees, shade and cooling centres to protect residents from extreme heat. Although these measures require space, maintenance and public funding, I believe their advantages clearly outweigh the disadvantages.

The primary benefit is health. An expanded urban tree canopy and shaded streets help neighbourhoods cool down, while cooling centres provide emergency protection for people without safe homes. These measures can reduce heatwave deaths and pressure on hospitals. Trees also improve air quality and make walking more comfortable. Cooling infrastructure can support social equality. Poorer areas often have fewer trees and hotter buildings, so targeted investment improves equitable access to protection. What makes urban greening valuable is that it combines climate adaptation with better public space.

The disadvantages are practical. Trees need water, soil and long-term care, while cooling centres require staff and clear opening plans. Poorly chosen species may damage pavements or fail during drought. Air-conditioned centres also increase electricity use. Only when maintenance is funded can planting programmes deliver lasting benefits. Cities should combine greenery with reflective materials, passive cooling and heat action plans. Data should identify vulnerable neighbourhoods, and public buildings should become reliable resilience hubs.

Cities should also protect outdoor workers through labour rules, water access and adjusted schedules. Heat adaptation cannot depend only on residents voluntarily visiting a centre after symptoms begin. Public-health monitoring can show whether interventions actually reduce illness across different neighbourhoods. The design should also avoid displacing vulnerable residents. Greener, cooler neighbourhoods may become more desirable and expensive, so housing protection must accompany climate investment. Otherwise, public money could improve an area while forcing the people most exposed to heat to move elsewhere, reproducing the same inequality in another district.

In conclusion: trees, shade and cooling centres are cost-effective measures with health and social benefits. Their advantages exceed their disadvantages when cities treat them as permanent infrastructure rather than temporary publicity projects.

TASK 2 · 4

Electricity grids are struggling to connect new renewable-energy projects. What problems does this cause, and what solutions should governments introduce?

Optional collocation bank
grid bottleneckshold backtransmission capacitypower-system flexibilitybuild outbattery storagedemand responseinstitutional capacityclimate mitigation
Model essay · 311 words

Renewable-energy projects are expanding faster than many electricity networks can connect them. This creates economic, technical and climate problems.

The first problem is delay. grid bottlenecks leave completed wind and solar projects waiting for access, increasing costs and hold back emissions reductions. Developers may abandon projects, while consumers continue paying for expensive fossil generation. Limited transmission capacity also prevents regions from sharing surplus electricity. A second problem is reliability. Electrification increases demand from transport, heating and industry. Without stronger networks and power-system flexibility, local congestion can raise prices or require renewable output to be wasted. What the transition lacks in many countries is not generation potential but infrastructure capable of moving it.

Governments should accelerate planning and coordinate national grid maps. They need to build out transmission, upgrade substations and train engineers. Permitting can be faster without removing environmental review if routes are assessed strategically rather than project by project. Markets should also reward battery storage, demand response and flexible consumption. Not only must grids become larger, but they must also become more digital and responsive. Regulators should ensure that network companies invest ahead of demand rather than waiting for congestion.

Grid reform must also address cost allocation. Communities hosting new lines should receive genuine consultation and local benefits, while network charges should not fall disproportionately on low-income households. Social legitimacy matters because infrastructure delayed by conflict delivers neither climate nor security benefits. Supply-chain planning is equally important. Transformers, cables and specialised engineers have long lead times, so emergency procurement cannot solve every delay. Governments should publish multi-year investment plans and support training. This improves institutional capacity and reduces the risk that ambitious climate targets exist on paper while projects remain physically unconnected.

In conclusion: weak grids delay climate targets, raise costs and threaten reliability. Long-term planning, faster connections and investment in flexibility are essential if renewable deployment is to continue.

TASK 2 · 5

Why is adaptation finance for developing countries insufficient? How can international climate funding become more effective?

Optional collocation bank
adaptation financestep upbuild inclimate mitigationclimate adaptationenergy securitygrid resiliencepower-system flexibilityrenewable deployment
Model essay · 313 words

Developing countries face growing heat, flood and drought risks, yet international adaptation finance remains far below estimated needs. This shortage reflects political incentives, debt constraints and weaknesses in the funding system.

Donor governments prefer visible projects and may prioritise mitigation investments that generate measurable emissions reductions. Adaptation benefits often appear as damage that did not occur, making them harder to demonstrate. Many vulnerable countries also lack the staff required to prepare complex applications. What the current system rewards is administrative capacity, not necessarily climate vulnerability. Finance is also fragmented. Short grants fund consultants and pilot projects but not long-term maintenance. Loans can increase debt even though poorer countries contributed little to historical emissions. Local governments and community organisations may receive little direct support.

Wealthy countries should step up grant-based finance and simplify access. Allocation should reflect vulnerability, exposure and institutional need. Funds should build in local participation, maintenance and transparent evaluation from the beginning. Multilateral banks can provide concessional finance for large infrastructure, while grants support warnings, health systems and community adaptation. Had earlier funding strengthened local institutions, more countries might now be able to implement plans rapidly.

Donors should publish how much funding reaches implementation rather than consultants, and programmes should report avoided losses, health outcomes and local capacity. Multi-year finance is crucial because resilience depends on maintenance, trusted institutions and learning across repeated climate events. Funding should also recognise that small-scale measures can deliver rapid benefits. Forecasting, water storage, health surveillance and local drainage may save more lives per dollar than one prestigious megaproject. National governments should allow cities and communities to combine such measures according to local risk rather than forcing every applicant into the same template.

In conclusion: adaptation finance is insufficient because benefits are politically invisible and access is administratively difficult. More grants, simpler procedures and stronger local ownership would make international funding both fairer and more effective.

Course index · Module 1 · Chapter 10

Academic English Studio · Topic 10 · Climate Mitigation, Adaptation and Energy Security


Saved automatically on this device.
